_meta.headTerm: failure to thrive in the elderly

h1Title: Failure to Thrive in the Elderly: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Failure to thrive in the elderly (adult FTT) is a clinical syndrome of progressive functional decline encompassing unintentional weight loss of at least 5% of body weight in 6-12 months, decreased appetite, reduced physical performance, and deteriorating cognitive function. It affects an estimated 5-35% of community-dwelling older adults, 25-40% of nursing home residents, and 50-60% of hospitalized geriatric patients, depending on the definition used. FTT is not a diagnosis but a clinical presentation requiring systematic identification of underlying drivers: malignancy, depression, dementia, polypharmacy, social isolation, and chronic organ failure each contribute in distinct proportions. Untreated FTT is associated with greater than 3-fold increased 12-month mortality.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 5-35% of community-dwelling adults aged 65+ and 25-40% of nursing home residents meet FTT criteria; prevalence increases sharply with age and frailty (PMID 15555341)
keyStats.demographics: Both sexes equally affected; prevalence rises from approximately 5% at age 65-74 to over 30% in adults aged 85+; social isolation, living alone, and poverty are independent risk multipliers
keyStats.avgAge: Most commonly identified in adults aged 75-85; rarely presents below age 65 in the absence of serious comorbidity
keyStats.globalCases: No single global count due to absence of universal diagnostic criteria; estimated 1.3-2 million new FTT presentations annually in the USA alone based on ICD-9/10 administrative data

definition: Failure to thrive in adults (ICD-10: R62.7 — 'Failure to thrive, adult') is a geriatric syndrome characterized by four core features that may occur alone or in combination: unintentional weight loss of 5% or more of body weight over 6-12 months, anorexia (reduced appetite lasting more than 4 weeks), sarcopenia with reduced physical performance (gait speed below 0.8 m/s or grip strength in the bottom quintile for sex and age), and cognitive or functional decline not accounted for by a single primary diagnosis. The syndrome is conceptually distinct from frailty (a deficit-accumulation state) and from cachexia (a catabolic wasting syndrome driven by cytokines in cancer or heart failure), though it overlaps substantially with both. The term 'failure to thrive' was originally a pediatric concept; in adult medicine it describes a heterogeneous syndrome that signals systemic physiological vulnerability rather than a single disease process. The underlying causes are diverse and typically multiple: cancer in 15-20%, depression in 30-40%, dementia in 20-30%, poorly controlled chronic disease in 25-30%, iatrogenic causes (polypharmacy, institutional feeding, adverse social environment) in 25-35%, and treatable nutritional deficiency in up to 25%. Because multiple overlapping causes coexist in most patients, geriatric comprehensive assessment — rather than organ-specific workup — is the appropriate diagnostic framework. Geriatricians, primary care physicians, and multidisciplinary geriatric teams manage FTT.

typesClassification.description: A dominant underlying medical condition — advanced cancer, end-stage heart failure, COPD, renal failure, or malabsorption — drives weight loss and functional decline. Systematic medical workup identifies and partially addresses the primary driver, though functional restoration may be limited.

Depression (in 30-40% of FTT patients), dementia (20-30%), and delirium superimposed on cognitive decline are the dominant drivers. Depression-associated FTT is the most reversible subtype; antidepressant therapy plus psychosocial intervention restores weight and function in up to 50-60% of cases.

Social isolation, bereavement, inability to shop or prepare food, poverty, and institutionalization all impair nutrition, physical activity, and cognitive engagement. These factors drive or perpetuate FTT independent of medical causes and are the most undertreated component.

Polypharmacy (five or more concurrent medications) is associated with anorexia, dysgeusia, xerostomia, and cognitive impairment in a substantial proportion of elderly patients. Medication review and deprescribing are therapeutic interventions in their own right.

primarySymptoms: Unintentional weight loss of 5% or more of body weight over 6-12 months, not explained by a known illness or intentional caloric restriction — the cardinal criterion for FTT in most clinical definitions.
Anorexia — markedly reduced appetite lasting more than 4 weeks, often described as a complete lack of interest in food rather than nausea or physical inability to eat.
Progressive reduction in activities of daily living (ADLs): loss of ability to dress, bathe, transfer, toilet, or eat independently compared with the prior 6-month functional baseline.
Generalized weakness and fatigue disproportionate to the level of activity, often described as 'no energy' or exhaustion with minimal exertion.
Sarcopenia with visible muscle wasting in the temporalis, interosseous muscles of the hands, and thighs; grip strength in the lowest quintile for age and sex.
Slowed gait velocity below 0.8 m/s on a 4-metre timed walk, or inability to rise from a chair without arm support — functional movement markers correlating with adverse outcomes.
Impaired cognition out of proportion to prior baseline — new difficulties with orientation, short-term memory, or complex tasks such as medication management or bill payment.
Social withdrawal, loss of interest in family, friends, or previously enjoyed activities — may reflect depression, dementia, or simply the exhaustion of progressive physical decline.
Increased susceptibility to infections (pneumonia, UTIs) from nutritional immunosuppression and immobility.
Dehydration signs: concentrated urine, dry mucous membranes, orthostatic hypotension — often reflecting both inadequate fluid intake and impaired thirst mechanisms in aging.
earlyWarningSigns: Unintentional loss of 2-3% body weight over 3 months in an adult over 70 — below the classical 5% threshold but predictive of accelerating FTT in frail individuals.
Progressive meal-skipping or shrinking portion sizes reported by a caregiver or family member, even before weight change is clinically apparent.
Geriatric Depression Scale (GDS) score above 5 in a thin older adult — depression is the most reversible and most commonly overlooked FTT driver.
New falls or near-falls in the preceding 3 months alongside weight loss — the combination signals advanced physical deconditioning requiring urgent multidisciplinary assessment.
emergencySigns: Weight loss exceeding 10% of body weight in 3 months with haemodynamic instability (hypotension, tachycardia, orthostasis) — requires hospital admission for nutritional resuscitation and urgent investigation.
Severe dysphagia with aspiration risk leading to aspiration pneumonia — requires speech and language therapy assessment and modified diet or enteral feeding consideration.
Acute-on-chronic delirium superimposed on dementia in an FTT patient — a medical emergency that may signal sepsis, medication toxicity, metabolic derangement, or new vascular event.
Pressure ulcer developing in a bed-bound FTT patient — stage 3-4 ulceration signals critical nutritional and skin care failure requiring immediate intensive wound and nutrition management.

causes.description: Cancer accounts for 15-20% of newly diagnosed FTT in community elders. Cancers most commonly identified in FTT workup include lung, colorectal, gastric, pancreatic, haematological, and prostate cancer. Anorexia-cachexia from cancer-derived cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) drives weight loss independent of caloric intake.

Clinical depression is the most commonly identified and most reversible FTT driver, present in 30-40% of FTT patients. Depression suppresses appetite, impairs motivation to eat and exercise, and accelerates functional decline. Antidepressant therapy (SSRIs, mirtazapine) combined with psychosocial support restores weight in 50-60% of cases.

Alzheimer's disease, vascular dementia, and other dementias cause FTT through impaired recognition of hunger, forgetting to eat, apraxia affecting food preparation, and difficulty with chewing or swallowing in advanced stages. FTT in dementia signals moderate-to-advanced disease and is associated with 6-12 month median survival in end-stage Alzheimer's.

Approximately 25-35% of FTT presentations are iatrogenic. Medications causing anorexia include SSRIs, metformin, digoxin, opioids, spironolactone, and bisphosphonates. Drugs causing dysgeusia (distorted taste) include metronidazole, ACE inhibitors, and certain antibiotics. Anticholinergic burden causes dry mouth, dysphagia, and cognitive impairment.

Widowhood reduces daily caloric intake by an average of 400 kcal/day in the first 6 months. Social isolation independently predicts weight loss, physical deconditioning, and depression. Food insecurity among adults over 65 is associated with frailty, hospitalization, and mortality independent of medical comorbidity.

End-stage heart failure, COPD, chronic kidney disease, and cirrhosis cause protein-energy malnutrition through increased resting energy expenditure, reduced oral intake, malabsorption, and anorexigenic cytokine production. FTT in advanced organ failure is often the clinical presentation that prompts palliative care referral.

riskFactors.description: Risk doubles with each decade above 65; anorexia of ageing (reduced appetite from hormonal, sensory, and social changes) affects 15-30% of adults over 75 and is the physiological substrate on which pathological FTT develops.

Older adults living alone eat fewer meals and have lower mean daily caloric intake. Social isolation is independently associated with 1.5-2 times higher mortality risk over 5 years (PMID 26555371). Bereavement doubles depression risk.

Each additional medication above 5 increases anorexia risk by approximately 15%. Medication review and deprescribing in elderly patients with FTT reduces adverse drug reactions and improves appetite in 20-30% of cases.

Frailty (Fried criteria: ≥3 of 5 features) identifies patients whose physiological reserve is insufficient to respond to stressors; frail adults have 3-4 times higher FTT risk than robust age-matched peers.

Mild cognitive impairment precedes FTT in 20-30% of patients; dementia accelerates weight loss by approximately 1 kg/year above age-related loss. Dysphagia develops in 80% of severe Alzheimer's patients.

Untreated geriatric depression is present in 30-40% of FTT patients; it is modifiable with psychotherapy and pharmacotherapy. Mirtazapine offers dual antidepressant and appetite-stimulant benefit in FTT with depression.
Approximately 5-8% of US adults over 65 report food insecurity (USDA 2022); food-insecure elders consume 10-20% fewer daily calories and have higher rates of hospitalization.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 75-85 are the peak demographic for FTT diagnosis; institutionalized or nursing-home residents have 5-8 times higher prevalence than community-dwelling peers.
Women have slightly higher FTT prevalence in community settings (likely reflecting longer survival to advanced age); men in care homes have disproportionate rates related to comorbidity burden.
Low-income elders and those living in socioeconomic deprivation have 2-3 times higher FTT rates, reflecting food insecurity and reduced access to preventive care.
Ethnic minorities in Western countries may have under-recognized FTT due to barriers to healthcare access and cultural variation in age-related weight norms.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of FTT in the elderly follows the Comprehensive Geriatric Assessment (CGA) framework rather than a linear organ-system workup. Initial steps establish the presence of objective weight loss (using serial weight measurements, MUST score, or Mini Nutritional Assessment short form, MNA-SF) and screen for the four treatable categories: malignancy, depression, dementia, and social-environmental barriers. A thorough medication review is mandatory in every case. The history should cover appetite and weight trend, functional ADL trajectory, mood, cognition, social support, bereavement, and medication use. Physical examination assesses nutritional status (BMI, calf circumference, mid-arm muscle circumference), muscle strength (grip dynamometry), gait speed (4-metre timed walk), and signs of systemic disease. Validated screening tools include the Geriatric Depression Scale-15 (GDS-15), Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) or Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA), Short Physical Performance Battery (SPPB), and the Malnutrition Universal Screening Tool (MUST). Laboratory workup includes CBC, metabolic panel with albumin and prealbumin, TSH, vitamin B12, folate, 25-OH vitamin D, C-reactive protein, urinalysis, and age-appropriate cancer screening. CT chest-abdomen-pelvis is appropriate when malignancy has not been excluded. Echocardiography, pulmonary function tests, and renal imaging are directed by clinical findings. Social worker assessment of home situation, caregiver status, and financial resources is integral to the CGA.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Validated 6-item screening tool for malnutrition risk in older adults; score 0-7 indicates malnutrition, 8-11 at risk; guides dietary and nutritional support intensity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Completed by a nurse or clinician in 3-5 minutes; includes BMI, weight loss history, mobility, acute disease, neuropsychological problems, and dietary intake.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Objective assessment of muscle strength; low grip strength (below 27 kg in men, below 16 kg in women) meets EWGSOP2 criteria for sarcopenia and predicts adverse FTT outcomes
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient squeezes a handheld dynamometer at maximum effort three times with each hand. Takes 5 minutes. Results immediately available.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures physical performance; speed below 0.8 m/s identifies mobility limitation and predicts hospitalization, falls, and mortality
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient walks at comfortable pace over a 4-metre course; time is recorded with a stopwatch. Takes 2-3 minutes; performed in any clinic corridor.

diagnosticTests.purpose: 15-item validated depression screening tool for older adults; score 0-5 normal, 5-9 mild depression, 10-15 severe depression; avoids somatic items that may overlap with physical illness
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Self-completed or interviewer-administered in 5-7 minutes. Positive screen prompts clinical diagnostic interview for major depression.

diagnosticTests.test: Comprehensive blood panel (CBC, metabolic, TSH, B12, folate, 25-OH vitamin D, CRP, albumin)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for haematological malignancy, thyroid disease, nutritional deficiencies (B12, vitamin D, folate), renal or hepatic failure, and inflammation as FTT drivers
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; most results available within 24 hours. Albumin and prealbumin reflect nutritional status; CRP reflects systemic inflammation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for occult malignancy (lung, colorectal, pancreatic, lymphoma) in patients with unexplained weight loss after initial negative workup
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 20-30 minutes in a CT scanner with intravenous contrast. Detects malignancy in 15-20% of unexplained weight loss presentations in adults over 65.

treatmentOverview: FTT management is individualized and multicomponent, targeting all modifiable contributors identified by comprehensive geriatric assessment. Depression — the most reversible driver — is treated with SSRIs (sertraline 50-100 mg/day or escitalopram 5-10 mg/day) or mirtazapine (7.5-30 mg nightly, which has appetite-stimulant properties). Antidepressant response in elderly is assessed at 6-8 weeks. Nutritional support is a pillar of treatment: oral nutritional supplements providing 300-600 kcal/day in addition to regular meals improve BMI, grip strength, and physical performance in 4-12 weeks in trials of malnourished elders (ESPEN 2023 guidelines recommend supplementation for all hospitalized adults with malnutrition). Polypharmacy review targeting anticholinergic burden, appetite-suppressant medications, and unnecessary medications is a therapeutic intervention achieving appetite improvement in 20-30% of FTT patients. Resistance exercise with protein supplementation is the most evidence-based intervention for reversing sarcopenia, increasing gait speed, and improving functional independence. Specific nutritional deficiencies (vitamin D, B12, iron) are corrected with targeted supplementation. Social interventions — home meal delivery, day centre attendance, caregiver support, and social prescription — address environmental FTT drivers that medical therapy alone cannot correct. Malignancy-related FTT is managed with oncology-directed therapy where feasible and palliative care otherwise; megestrol acetate (400-800 mg/day) and mirtazapine can improve appetite and quality of life in cancer-related anorexia-cachexia. Dysphagia management by speech and language therapy with modified diet textures or nasogastric/PEG feeding preserves nutrition in severe cases.

medicalTreatments.name: SSRIs (sertraline 50 mg/day or escitalopram 5-10 mg/day) for depression-related FTT
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for depression-driven FTT; SSRIs are preferred in elderly for lower anticholinergic burden and safer cardiac profile. Assess response at 6-8 weeks; if inadequate, consider mirtazapine, which combines antidepressant and appetite-stimulant properties.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Antidepressant response rates 50-65% in late-life depression; weight and functional improvement correlates with mood response in 50-60% of FTT patients with concurrent depression (PMID 17401152)

medicalTreatments.name: Mirtazapine (7.5-30 mg nightly)
medicalTreatments.description: Noradrenergic and specific serotonergic antidepressant with H1 antihistamine-mediated appetite stimulation; preferred in FTT patients with co-existing depression and poor appetite. Start at 7.5 mg to minimize falls risk from sedation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Appetite improvement in 40-60% and weight gain 1-2 kg at 12 weeks in elderly FTT with depression; mean weight gain 1.5 kg versus 0.3 kg with SSRI in a head-to-head comparison

medicalTreatments.name: Oral nutritional supplements (300-600 kcal/day in addition to meals)
medicalTreatments.description: High-calorie, high-protein liquid or pudding supplements (Ensure, Fortisip, Resource) taken between meals add 300-600 kcal/day without reducing regular meal intake. ESPEN 2023 guidelines recommend supplementation for all malnourished hospitalized elders.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analysis of 36 RCTs: ONS reduced all-cause mortality by 21% (RR 0.79), complications by 26%, and re-admission by 24% in malnourished hospitalized older adults (PMID 24400688)

medicalTreatments.name: Vitamin D supplementation (800-2,000 IU/day) with calcium
medicalTreatments.description: Vitamin D deficiency is present in 40-60% of FTT patients and contributes to muscle weakness, falls, and immune impairment. Target serum 25-OH vitamin D above 50 nmol/L. Monthly high-dose regimens (50,000 IU) achieve faster repletion in those with severe deficiency.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Vitamin D supplementation in deficient elders reduces falls by 20-30% and improves gait speed and muscle strength in meta-analyses (PMID 19124985)

medicalTreatments.name: Megestrol acetate (400-800 mg/day) for cancer-related anorexia
medicalTreatments.description: Progestational agent that stimulates appetite via neuropeptide Y and orexin pathways; used in FTT with cancer-related anorexia-cachexia when prognosis justifies symptom-focused therapy. Risk includes thromboembolic events and adrenal suppression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Appetite improvement in 60-80% and weight gain 1.5-3 kg at 12 weeks, but predominantly fat mass gain rather than lean mass (PMID 16163361); appropriate for symptom management in advanced cancer

alternativeTreatments.name: Resistance exercise with protein supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Progressive resistance exercise (3 sessions/week for 12 weeks) combined with protein supplementation (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) is the most evidence-based intervention for reversing sarcopenia in FTT; improves gait speed, grip strength, and ADL independence in 3-6 months. The SENATOR-ONTOP systematic review confirmed superiority over nutrition or exercise alone (PMID 28756034).

alternativeTreatments.name: Social prescription and day centre attendance
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured social engagement — day centre attendance, befriending services, community meal programs — addresses social isolation and depression in FTT. A 2020 Cochrane review found social contact interventions reduced depression scores and improved nutritional intake in isolated older adults.

whySeeSpecialist: A comprehensive geriatric assessment by a geriatrician or geriatric team is indicated when FTT is identified or suspected in any adult over 70. Geriatric teams detect reversible contributors (depression, polypharmacy, nutritional deficiency, social isolation) that organ-specific specialists often miss. Referral to dietetics, speech and language therapy, occupational therapy, physiotherapy, and social work should accompany geriatrician review.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a geriatrician at a hospital or clinic offering comprehensive geriatric assessment, ideally in a memory and frailty unit. Community geriatric teams increasingly provide home-based CGA for patients unable to attend hospital. GP practices with a named geriatric frailty lead are appropriate for initial assessment and community management.
hospitalCriteria: Geriatric inpatient unit with multidisciplinary comprehensive geriatric assessment capability
Dietetic service with experience in elderly nutritional rehabilitation
Speech and language therapy for dysphagia assessment and management
Occupational therapy for ADL assessment and home adaptation
Physiotherapy for resistance exercise prescription and fall prevention
keyFacilities: Geriatric day hospitals for outpatient CGA and rehabilitation
Memory clinics for FTT with dominant cognitive impairment
Community nursing and home care services for frail homebound elders
preventionStrategies: Conduct annual malnutrition and frailty screening (MNA-SF, Fried Phenotype) in all community-dwelling adults over 70 during routine primary care visits.
Review the medication list at every appointment for adults taking five or more drugs; consider structured polypharmacy review to identify appetite-suppressant and cognitively impairing agents.
Promote social engagement through community participation, day centres, and befriending services — social isolation independently drives FTT independent of medical causes.
Prescribe resistance exercise and ensure protein intake of 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day from middle age onwards to preserve muscle mass and delay sarcopenia.
Screen for and treat depression in older adults using validated tools at primary care visits; late-life depression doubles FTT risk and is highly reversible with treatment.
lifestyleModifications: Eat three meals per day plus 2 protein-rich snacks; aim for 1.2-1.5 g protein/kg body weight per day from eggs, dairy, fish, legumes, and lean meat.
Participate in group meals where possible (community dining, care home dining room, family meals) — social eating increases daily caloric intake by 20-30% versus eating alone.
Perform resistance exercises (chair squats, wall press-ups, light weights) at least 3 days per week to counter sarcopenia and maintain functional independence.
Take vitamin D 800-2,000 IU/day unless already documented above 50 nmol/L — deficiency is present in 40-60% of elderly adults in temperate climates.
Report reduced appetite or unintentional weight loss to the GP or care team within 4 weeks; early identification dramatically improves FTT reversibility.
Maintain adequate fluid intake of 1.5-2 L daily; dehydration worsens confusion, weakness, and constipation that compound FTT.

recommended: Protein-dense foods at every meal: eggs, chicken, fish, Greek yoghurt, cottage cheese, legumes — aiming 25-30 g protein per meal to maximize muscle protein synthesis
Fortified milk (full-fat milk with 4 tablespoons of skimmed milk powder per pint) to increase caloric and protein density without increasing volume
Oral nutritional supplements (Fortisip, Ensure, Complan) between meals as prescribed by a dietitian to meet caloric targets
Omega-3 fatty acid-rich foods (oily fish, walnuts, flaxseed) for anti-inflammatory properties and muscle anabolic signalling
avoid: Ultra-low-fat or very-low-calorie diets — caloric restriction is inappropriate in FTT; caloric density should be maximized
Fibre-heavy foods that cause early satiety if total food intake is already below requirements — supplement fibre separately
Large volumes of low-calorie fluids (tea, water) immediately before meals, displacing calorie-dense food intake
Medications with appetite-suppressant side-effects unless clinically indispensable — discuss substitution with prescribing physician

exerciseGuidelines: Progressive resistance exercise 3 days per week combined with protein supplementation is the gold-standard intervention for sarcopenia in FTT. Start with chair exercises, wall press-ups, and resistance bands progressing to free weights under physiotherapy supervision. Aerobic activity (walking 20-30 minutes daily) improves cardiovascular function and depression. Balance training (tandem walking, single-leg stand) reduces falls risk. Exercise prescription should be tailored to functional status by a physiotherapist.
dailyManagement: Weigh weekly on the same scale, at the same time of day, in similar clothing — a fall of more than 2% over 4 weeks warrants clinician notification.
Complete a daily food and fluid diary for review by the dietitian; most elderly patients under-report actual intake when asked without a diary.
Take prescribed nutritional supplements between meals rather than instead of meals to maximize additive caloric benefit.
Attend all physiotherapy and occupational therapy appointments — functional decline in FTT is partially but meaningfully reversible with consistent rehabilitation.
Ensure a caregiver or family member reviews medications with the GP every 6-12 months to identify and deprescribe appetite-suppressant agents.
prognosis: Prognosis in elderly FTT depends critically on underlying cause and reversibility. Depression-related FTT treated with antidepressants and social support achieves weight restoration and functional improvement in 50-60% of patients within 3-6 months. Malnutrition-related FTT corrected with nutritional support and resistance exercise improves muscle mass, gait speed, and ADL scores in 12-24 weeks of structured intervention. However, FTT from advanced malignancy, end-stage organ failure, or severe dementia has a poor prognosis: median survival is 6-12 months in multiple observational studies (PMID 15555341). One-year mortality in community FTT is approximately 20-30%; in hospitalized elders with FTT, 30-day mortality reaches 10-15% and 1-year mortality 25-40%. Early comprehensive geriatric assessment and targeted multidisciplinary intervention substantially improve outcomes in reversible FTT but cannot alter the trajectory of end-stage disease. Palliative care goals, advance care planning, and goals-of-care conversations are integral to FTT management in the irreversible subset.
recoveryTimeline: Nutritional rehabilitation in community FTT: 4-8 weeks for measurable weight gain; 12-16 weeks for grip strength and gait improvement. Antidepressant response: 6-8 weeks for mood; weight recovery 3-6 months. Functional rehabilitation (physiotherapy): gait speed improvement within 8-12 weeks; ADL improvement within 12-24 weeks. Polypharmacy deprescribing: appetite improvement within 2-4 weeks for many iatrogenic causes.
complications: Sarcopenic frailty with falls and fractures — unintentional weight loss and sarcopenia increase hip fracture risk 2-4 times; vitamin D and calcium supplementation combined with exercise reduces fracture incidence.
Pressure ulcers from combination of malnutrition, reduced mobility, and impaired immune function — protein and calorie supplementation is the primary prevention and treatment.
Increased susceptibility to pneumonia and UTIs from malnutrition-related immunosuppression — every 1 g/dL fall in albumin below 3.5 g/dL approximately doubles infection complication risk.
Delirium superimposed on dementia — dehydration, infection, and metabolic derangements in FTT patients with cognitive impairment precipitate delirium in up to 30% of hospitalizations.

Hospital-acquired deconditioning — each day of bed rest in a frail elder results in approximately 1-3% loss of lower extremity strength; targeted rehabilitation in hospital prevents permanent decline.

End-of-life transition — FTT in advanced dementia or malignancy signals terminal decline; goals-of-care conversations and palliative care referral should occur early in the FTT course when prognosis is poor.

supportResources.name: British Geriatrics Society — Comprehensive Geriatric Assessment
supportResources.description: Free CGA toolkit for primary care practitioners covering frailty, malnutrition, falls, and cognitive impairment in older adults.

supportResources.name: National Council on Aging — Falls & Malnutrition Resources
supportResources.description: US-focused patient resources on nutrition, falls prevention, and healthy ageing programs for older adults.

supportResources.name: AGS Foundation for Health in Aging — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: American Geriatrics Society patient portal with resources on frailty, weight loss, polypharmacy, and caregiver support for older adults.

confusedWithConditions.name: Frailty Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Frailty is a state of accumulated physiological deficit characterized by exhaustion, weakness, slow gait, low activity, and unintentional weight loss meeting at least 3 of 5 Fried criteria. FTT is a broader clinical presentation requiring causal investigation; many frail patients also meet FTT criteria, but FTT emphasizes active investigation for reversible drivers including depression, malignancy, and medication causes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Geriatric Malnutrition
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Malnutrition is a specific nutritional diagnosis defined by GLIM criteria (weight loss, low BMI, or reduced muscle mass plus causal factor). FTT encompasses malnutrition but adds cognitive, functional, and social decline. Malnutrition may be managed by dietetics alone; FTT requires multidisciplinary comprehensive geriatric assessment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Alzheimer's Disease and Dementia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dementia causes cognitive decline and eventually FTT as a complication in moderate-to-advanced stages. FTT as a presenting problem in an older adult may be the first indication of previously unrecognized dementia. Cognitive screening (MoCA, MMSE) is standard in FTT workup; however, FTT has many non-dementia causes that must be investigated before attributing decline to cognitive disease alone.

confusedWithConditions.name: Geriatric Depression
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Geriatric depression frequently presents without prominent low mood; instead, reduced appetite, weight loss, fatigue, and social withdrawal dominate — a syndrome easily labelled as 'failure to thrive'. The Geriatric Depression Scale distinguishes depression from other FTT causes. Depression-related FTT is the most reversible subtype, responding in 50-60% of cases to antidepressants and psychosocial support.

relatedConditions.name: Frailty Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: FTT and frailty overlap substantially; most patients meeting FTT criteria also have frailty; prevention and management strategies are shared.

relatedConditions.name: Falls in the Elderly
relatedConditions.relevance: Sarcopenia, muscle weakness, and slowed gait velocity in FTT drive falls risk; FTT management with resistance exercise reduces falls incidence.

relatedConditions.name: Geriatric Malnutrition
relatedConditions.relevance: Malnutrition is present in 40-60% of FTT cases; nutritional supplementation and dietetic management are core FTT interventions.

relatedConditions.name: Delirium in the Elderly
relatedConditions.relevance: Delirium is a frequent complication of FTT during hospital admission from dehydration, infection, and metabolic derangements in nutritionally depleted elders.

relatedConditions.name: Polypharmacy Complications
relatedConditions.relevance: Polypharmacy causes or perpetuates FTT through anorexia, dysgeusia, cognitive impairment, and falls; systematic deprescribing is a therapeutic component of FTT management.

faqs.question: What is failure to thrive in the elderly?
faqs.answer: Failure to thrive in the elderly is a geriatric syndrome combining unintentional weight loss of 5% or more in 6-12 months, reduced appetite, progressive physical decline, and worsening cognitive or functional ability. It is not a single diagnosis but a clinical presentation requiring systematic investigation of underlying causes including depression, malignancy, dementia, and polypharmacy.

faqs.question: What causes failure to thrive in older adults?
faqs.answer: The most common causes are depression (30-40%), dementia (20-30%), occult malignancy (15-20%), polypharmacy side-effects (25-35%), poorly controlled chronic illness, and social isolation. Most patients have more than one contributing cause, which is why comprehensive geriatric assessment is recommended over single-organ investigations.

faqs.question: How is failure to thrive diagnosed in elderly patients?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis uses comprehensive geriatric assessment: validated tools including the Mini Nutritional Assessment, Geriatric Depression Scale, and Montreal Cognitive Assessment, combined with grip strength, gait speed testing, full blood work, and CT imaging for occult malignancy. Social and functional history from caregivers is essential.

faqs.question: Is failure to thrive reversible in the elderly?
faqs.answer: Reversibility depends on cause. Depression-related FTT is the most reversible, with 50-60% of patients regaining weight and function with antidepressants and social support. Nutritional deficiency and polypharmacy causes are highly treatable. FTT from advanced malignancy or end-stage organ failure has a poor prognosis with a median survival of 6-12 months.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for elderly failure to thrive?
faqs.answer: One-year mortality in community failure to thrive is approximately 20-30%; in hospitalized elders it reaches 25-40%. Early identification and comprehensive geriatric assessment substantially improve outcomes for reversible causes. FTT with unresectable malignancy or severe dementia signals terminal decline requiring palliative care planning.

faqs.question: How is depression related to failure to thrive in the elderly?
faqs.answer: Depression is present in 30-40% of elderly FTT patients and is the single most reversible contributing cause. In older adults, depression often presents as weight loss, fatigue, and withdrawal rather than low mood. Antidepressants, particularly mirtazapine or SSRIs, restore appetite and function in 50-60% of cases within 3-6 months.

faqs.question: What is the difference between frailty and failure to thrive?
faqs.answer: Frailty is a physiological state measured by the Fried phenotype — exhaustion, weakness, slow gait, low activity, and weight loss. Failure to thrive is a broader clinical syndrome that includes cognitive and functional decline with active investigation for reversible causes. Many FTT patients also meet frailty criteria, but FTT emphasizes causal diagnosis and intervention.

faqs.question: How does polypharmacy cause failure to thrive?
faqs.answer: Polypharmacy — five or more concurrent medications — commonly causes anorexia, dysgeusia (altered taste), dry mouth, nausea, and cognitive impairment in older adults. SSRIs, digoxin, metformin, opioids, and anticholinergics are frequent offenders. Medication review and deprescribing improve appetite and cognition in 20-30% of FTT patients.

faqs.question: What nutritional supplements help with failure to thrive?
faqs.answer: Oral nutritional supplements providing 300-600 additional calories per day between meals are recommended by ESPEN 2023 guidelines for malnourished older adults. Protein-enriched formulas (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day total protein) preserve muscle mass. Vitamin D (800-2,000 IU/day) corrects deficiency present in 40-60% of FTT patients and improves muscle strength.

faqs.question: Can exercise reverse failure to thrive?
faqs.answer: Yes. Progressive resistance exercise 3 times per week, combined with protein supplementation, is the best-evidenced intervention for reversing sarcopenia and improving gait speed, grip strength, and daily activity in FTT patients. Functional improvements are measurable within 8-12 weeks of consistent supervised exercise.

faqs.question: When should a geriatrician be involved in failure to thrive?
faqs.answer: A geriatrician should be involved in all adults over 70 with unintentional weight loss of 5% or more, declining functional ability, new cognitive symptoms, or unexplained appetite reduction lasting more than 4 weeks. Comprehensive geriatric assessment identifies treatable contributors that organ-specific specialists are likely to miss.

faqs.question: Is failure to thrive a sign of dying in the elderly?
faqs.answer: In advanced malignancy or late-stage dementia, FTT is a recognized preterminal syndrome with median survival of 6-12 months. However, in many older adults FTT is caused by treatable depression, nutritional deficiency, or polypharmacy and is fully or partially reversible. Prognosis must be assessed individually after thorough evaluation.

faqs.question: How much weight loss is significant in an older adult?
faqs.answer: Unintentional weight loss of 5% of body weight in 6-12 months is clinically significant and warrants full investigation. A loss of 10% or more in 6 months carries greater than 3-fold increased 12-month mortality risk. Even 2-3% unintentional loss over 3 months in a frail elder warrants assessment.

faqs.question: What social factors contribute to failure to thrive?
faqs.answer: Social isolation, bereavement, food insecurity, inability to shop or cook, and loss of appetite after losing a spouse each independently reduce caloric intake and drive FTT. Widowhood reduces daily caloric intake by approximately 400 kcal/day in the first 6 months. Social prescribing, meal delivery, and day centre attendance are evidence-based interventions.

faqs.question: How is failure to thrive different from anorexia nervosa?
faqs.answer: Anorexia nervosa is a psychiatric eating disorder with specific diagnostic criteria (intense fear of weight gain, body image disturbance) occurring predominantly in young women. Elderly failure to thrive involves loss of appetite without deliberate food restriction, driven by medical, social, or medication causes. The two conditions rarely coexist and require entirely different management.

faqs.question: What is mirtazapine used for in failure to thrive?
faqs.answer: Mirtazapine is an antidepressant with appetite-stimulant properties from histamine H1 receptor blockade. Prescribed at 7.5-30 mg nightly, it improves both depression and appetite in FTT patients with concurrent low mood. It causes weight gain of 1-2 kg at 12 weeks in elderly patients with depression and anorexia.

simpleName: Failure to thrive in the elderly (adult FTT, geriatric decline syndrome)
symptomKeywords: aged person sleeping more eating less
metaTitle: Elderly Failure to Thrive: Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Elderly failure to thrive: weight loss, reduced appetite, and functional decline. Causes include depression, dementia, and polypharmacy. Treatment reviewed.
